text: "Addict" has become a profit driven word used by corporate rehab to convince perfectly capable ppl into thinking their powerless and need their help. Some do, but these ppl often have clear addictive personalities or compulsion issues from a number of possible factors. Still, not all these ppl are addicts either. I have a very complex dysphoric mania w/ major depression disorder that is medication resistant, but I keep the monkey off my back with one tool... It's going to sound cheesy, but it's self-love. You have got to learn how to love your fucked up self. Thankfully for me it was confrontation, psychedelics, and patience. Therapy can also be helpful, but expensive and hard to find a good one. Lots of people are self-medicating. These condescending rehab types jump on here trying to push their propagandized guilt onto everyone else, to support their own reality. This is because they were torn down and not built up. And I dabble still once or twice a month. You go 4 weeks feeling nothing but dysphoria (dread) and see what you do. I sometimes need that break. But I also got myself off all my ER pain medication, on my own and by my own fruition, when I could have continued it indefinitely. When I started turning down Dilaudid, I knew I'd finally gotten somewhere. In the past I would have taken numerous different drugs to feel some euphoria, but they'd often make matters worse, so the juice wasn't worth the squeeze. And because I actually love myself, I stopped putting myself thru that shit. So no, not everyone on here thinks they're not a druggie and actually are. There's just a bunch of brainwashed types that don't know their own strengths and probably never will.

text: Honestly, drugs. Lol I have low dose mirtazapine and hydroxyzine. I've tried all the "tricks" and they just don't work, especially when my anxiety is bad. And if I don't sleep, the anxiety gets even worse, so if becomes a vicious cycle. But if you want to go the non drug route, before I had issues with sleep anxiety, reading used to always make me really sleepy. So id read before bed. The DARE app has some sleep audio and relaxing sounds. Some people do counting. Count to 100 in your head slowly. If you make it to 100, start over. For the people this works for, they usually don't make it to 100. Gives you something to occupy your thoughts. If you get distracted with your thoughts, just start where you left off. You could also try something over the counter. Olly makes a sleep aid that might be worth a try to help you relax. My husband falls asleep in like 10 mins and I'm so jealous of him!

text: Well, this is complicated topic. But I try to simplify my answer. (This became a TED-talk ?? Take a cup of tea and enjoy my motivational rant??) YES! But it depends. On many factors tho. And should you even do it, even if you could self-medicate safely? Let's tackle this one by one. Firstly: What do we mean when we talk about a drug? I attach a great quote from Midnight Gospel show. It goes something like: "Drugs are not good or bad. They just are. They are substances/chemicals created by nature or human. Problem is not the substance itself, but peoples relation to them." That being said, are we talking about drug or medicine. What defines it? I have ADHD. If I go and buy Concerta/Adderall from the streets to self medicate, it is considered as a drug. But if doctor prescribes me the same pill, it is considered a medicine. Legally, that is. Even if the intent of use would be medicating. Same with weed. Cannabis needs a lot of more studies, but it is highly likely (and studied) that many people use cannabis to self-medication. Not even on purpose. Sometimes people use it recreationally, it feels good in moderation and even helps on problems, then they take it too much and symptoms worsen. Also creating addiction. Taking too much of anything, being it even ADHD meds messes you up. Risks and consequences. There are many levels on many drugs. But can you learn how to use it correctly? Sure, people do it all the time. Even most responsible adults have started their youth with playing with booze, vomiting and messing up. Learning from their mistakes, finding the balance and moderation. Same goes with many drugs. And also "actual" medicine or "prescription drugs" . (not gonna start a fight at this, drugs is so umbrella term for many things like for example psychedelics) You can also get addicted to medicine that is described for you, in a way. Like: Does the medicine do what it is supposed to do? Does it help with the problem like it should, improving your life? ADHD is like that. A medicine that could improve your life quality, since not treated or medicated could make you a total train wreck and ruin your life in many ways. Is it bad to be addicted to something that helps you live your life better? Is it bad to be dependent of the drug? Some people who get heart transplant, need to take medication so that their bodies don't reject the new heart?? Are they depended on it? Or addicted. Well yeah, kinda. But if they don't take it, they die. (and I know this is exaggerating, but you get the point) Now last thing: Is it good to be dependent on drug, even if it helps you? To be addicted to something that improves your life. Yes, and no. If the medicine helps you, it's good thing. That's what it's supposed to do. But is it the only option? Propably not. You could use the benefit of a drug to stabilize you and improve your life in a manner, so that you can do other improvements as well. ADHD meds are only half the solution. If you use meds but end them, many symptoms come back aprox in 6 months, give or take. Putting you back to base line, unless you used them to make changes that aid in future. 6-8 weeks of cognitive psychotherapy where you learn life skills and habits, scheduling, order, structure, managing symptoms etc helps even better, and can aid you up for rest of your life. Not to talk about meditation (stoic ????? for the win) excercise, healthy diet, vitamins, water and better life choices in general. And you you can combine the both, you are on a road to victory ?? Our environment affects our life in many ways. It's hard, I'm not saying that. But discomfort also creates strength?? And you should do the therapy if you think that would help. "If someone you loved were in exactly the same position where you are now, what would you advice?" If you would advice to get help, then you know the answer: Go get it. To healthcare and loved ones, friends you trust. There's no shame to ask help if needed. But be merciful of yourself aswell. Change takes time. Do some stoic meditations, take a 5min walk in fresh air, make a cup of tea/coffee, journal your racing thoughts in a journal (Brain dump, everything what stresses) and Take. Care. Of. Yourself. And relax. Give permission for that to yourself. Do something that makes you happy. That's a start! Love yourself first, so you can love others?? You feel so much better when you do it. And then, if you need to self medicate, do it. But ask yourself is it the only option you can do. What else could I do to feel better? Should I test what it likes to be sober, even just for a while. Few days, week? A month. You don't need to do it forever, just so you can do some inner growth and learn moderation, self-reflecting your past mistakes and what went right and worked, so you won't fail again so easily. People use drugs because... Drugs are fun?? But if they control your life, instead of you being in control, then you should do some self help and work with yourself. Sometimes it's fun to escape a reality a bit. But we still live in this reality, better make best of it and try to enjoy what life offers. Being grateful of small things and loved ones we have, instead what we don't have. There will always be things that stress you, things that don't go as planned, creating anxiety and depression. Meds can help to ease the edge when you are in a bad spot. But they are not ending solution. You learn ways to fight and tolerate them better and get back up. Even after you quit meds, you have learned ways to deal your problems better. Better than before atleast. That's growth. Day by day?? Funny thing about anxiety is, that it never actually ends. You just get used to it, and the topics of it change. New sources of anxiety come and go. But you grow better. That is actually good thing to know. "We can't affect on our environment. Only how we react to it ourselves" I know you can learn to react better?? Just find your own way to do it. Creating a better version of yourself: To yourself. Much love, stay safe???????
predicted_span: Sometimes people use it recreationally, it feels good in moderation and even helps on problems, then they take it too much and symptoms worsen. Also creating addiction.

text: You were very lucky, that's all. If you have had previous experience with diazepam, it's less of a miracle. The human body can build incredible tolerance, but at the limit it's highly dependent on many variable factors so we just can't say anything for sure. I used to feed my opiate addiction by taking OTC pills with codeine and ibuprofen. The limit for these is like 12 a day, and people regularly commit suicide by taking 30 at once. it's a very painful way to go that takes several days of intense pain. However, I was taking more than 100 of these a day for several years, until I got into a methodone programme. By all medical science I should have died a couple of times every damn month. I never even got particularly sick. And when I got a sore stomach I'd just take more painkillers. My point is that you survived because you are lucky. That's about it. Don't be such an idiot.
predicted_span: You were very lucky, that's all.

text: Actually, idgaf. I'll bite. Sorry for wall of text, I feel like context is valuable. At age 6, I started on Ritalin. My teacher at the time said it was night and day difference, but when she found out I was being "medicated" she was like "Oh no! That's not what I wanted when I threatened to kick your kid out of school!" But the meds worked great, and didn't hurt me at all. My social life improved... marginally, though I still had behavioral issues like speaking out of turn and disruptively banging my head on the desk (3rd grade). Huge improvement, still wasn't easy. When I was 15, I had a crush on a girl who said "my parents put me on ADHD medication once, but I didn't like it. Do you need it? Maybe you can stop?" So I stopped. Cold turkey, the middle of freshman year. Absolutely no problems with withdrawl or anything. I barely noticed. I had apparently developed the study skills and determination to work my way through problems, so I went through Highschool and college without any meds! Then I started working, and 6 years later, it's 2011, and I had been working Help Desk for 2 years. I'd be on the phone with a customer, and they would put me on hold... so I'd start reading an article on Reddit, and then the person would come back to the phone, and I'd be like "Aaah! Who are you, and why are we talking?" One day I read an article about Adult ADHD, and I realized ... Oh, I still have it. Doctor was like "Duh. It doesn't go away." So I started taking Adderall this time. I didn't notice a difference, but within a week my boss says I've been doing great work. Which was weird, because I had previously thought my work was already the best on the team, but whatever. I was ready to advance my career, so I started pursuing Cyber Security training certifications after work. I was poor, so I pirated all the books and did self-study. One year later, I'm working full-time cyber. It hasn't been an easy journey for me, but I've got a good family, a good career, and a home. Also a permanent case of impostor syndrome.

text: Not a doctor, but someone who went through a lot of really hard stuff at a young age. I had a parent I loved fiercely but who couldn't provide me a safe or stable environment. I felt like I was tough and could take care of myself, so I wasn't honest with doctors. Deep down, I thought people would judge me and blame me. I say this to tell you, I understand you. I've been you. By 12, I was self-medicating with marijuana and alcohol. I lied to doctors. By 13, I was self-harming and had a failed suicide attempt. I lied to doctors. At 14, I developed an eating disorder. I lied to doctors. Bad people smell trauma like sharks smell blood in the water. At 16, I was raped by someone I loved and trusted. I was so used to handling things on my own that I didn't anybody. My late teens and early 20s were a blur of alcohol, marijuana, cocaine, Vicodin, abusive relationships, and promiscuity. I lied to doctors. I got good at cramming all the bad feelings and memories into little boxes in my mind. White noise took the place of my emotions. I was numb. New bad things would happen or ugly memories would resurface, and they would get crammed into their own boxes. I lied to doctors. Then a few years ago, I went through a big traumatic event that broke open all those boxes at once. All the bad came back at the same time. I was unprepared. I was drowning. I stopped lying to doctors. One of the doctors I told changed my life. I'm 38 years old and only recently realized how connected all the bad things were. I lied to doctors because deep down I knew I wasn't actually ok and something really was wrong (otherwise why would I have lied?). I was way too young to carry all that hurt and responsibility. It wasn't okay that I was put in that position, no matter how much I loved my parent. I finally got real help, and I desperately wish I could go back to that 12 year old me and convince her to be honest to doctors so she could have gotten real help instead. Please be honest with your doctors. They're not going to judge you or think you're a bad kid. They're going give you the support, compassion, and help you truly need.

text: Hello, I thought I would share my experience as someone who has been working on this for a while. Got my diagnosis last year, figured out I had it about 4 years ago. Still not medicated, due to ADHD medication shortages in UK. It sounds like you're talking about the executive dysfunction we all love to hate. I struggle with it on the daily as I'm sure many others do; with things I want to do, enjoy doing, don't want to do, and NEED to do to keep my life in order. Not wanting to be presumptive but it sounds like you need some ADHD tools, to use with your medication to feel better about how you spend your time. As I'm still not medicated I've had to rearrange my life around my ADHD and it has helped. I have managed to get into a few hobbies and stick with them, and be much more productive, but only through A LOT of mindfulness training to tackle my negative thoughts and the executive function issues. But also, you have ADHD. It's gonna try and get in the way sometimes; you can forgive yourself for not being like "other people". That doesn't mean you can't live a more full life and access things that interest you. I have spent a lot of time learning about ADHD, and trying to learn about myself Another thing that's helped me is pursuit of what I actually enjoy doing and things that encourage my curiosity, and changing my life routine to suit me. A big thing that helps me is I've developed a *tick list* or like a menu of things to do. It helps me not veg in a chair for hours by accident or play video games all day. On the menu there is, the chores that need to be done, the exercise options I have, and the work that needs to be done and then options for me to do to chill out. I don't have to do everything on the list, it's more of an aid to memory for me to go "Oh yeah, I've been wanting to read that book, do some drawing, go to the pottery studio, make plans with that friend, or maybe I should do a yoga class on YouTube because I feel like I have a small colony of bees living in my forehead and I can feel colours" This helps me, because when I do have motivation, I find it easy to get overwhelmed by choice because ADHD brain is running too fast and wants to do all of the ideas *right now* My brain used to get overwhelmed, cry, and go back to factory settings, and then play 6 hours of repetitive PlayStation or something because it was an escape from feeling funky. Then I'd feel guilty for wasting my life. Instead I have a reminder of things I can build my day around. So I have some structure, but it's not rigid, but there's a rhythm to it. More of a flexi routine. And the more you work at a flexi routine the more things will stick Some things won't stick or work for you, if they don't, bin them off it's your life I'm combined type and a very socially active life seems to suit me, with exercise built in, and some creativity built in too. But also, the inattentive part of me wants to chill out, space away, and have stability and they're very difficult to reconcile. I'm also in the last couple months of studying law part time and working full time so my life is all over the place rn and executive function has a massive strop whenever I need to study ._. Maybe you can carve out some time to think about what you think is a good life, and the kind of you that you want to spend time being? And what things you can do to be more like that person right now? Another thing; just a general discipline thing; if you're struggling to kick a bad habit, you have to remove temptation from yourself to do that habit. If an alcoholic has alcohol in their house, they will get drunk. Speaking as someone who does not buy alcohol to keep in their house anymore because I used to self medicate all the time. If you feel stuck on social media for hours and hours a day, maybe it's time to delete the apps off your phone, just for a while. Not in a cut yourself off from your people kind of way. But in a, "I need a change, and I'm taking that step" kind of way. I deleted instagram for 6 months and instagram reels have lost their power over me. Now I just use it to talk to a few friends and post pictures of my cat and random things. Focus apps are also good - like Flora; it's free and you can set it so if you break your focus you pay the developers 50 pence or whatever to give yourself consequences and urgency instead of it just being an annoying thing that locks you out of Reddit for a couple hours. You can also set permitted apps - e.g my flora only lets me use Spotify and that's it. Finally a shoutout to the ADHD Adults Podcast because they taught me more about my condition, and the tendencies associated with ADHD people, than the actual fucking doctors did. I hate podcasts but I love theirs because it's a self described "chaotic squirrel rave" run by ADHD people, for ADHD people and is really good for learning about the behaviours that are common in people with brains like yours, so you can figure out more about how you work yourself. That's on Spotify too and probably other places. You have my sympathy that you're struggling. But hey at least you're aware that something isn't working for you anymore. Next step is exploring how you can change it so you feel more content and at peace with yourself whatever that looks like for you

text: I'm 48F and definitely yes. I feel like I never drink enough water. And if you are planning on Adderall for life, there are risks to long-term usage. I'm curious about your dosage? Not saying it's normal to be dehydrated, but I care about our kidneys ?? I have these symptoms when I'm not drinking enough water: Increased thirst Dry mouth and throat Dark yellow urine Feeling unusually tired or lethargic Dizziness or lightheadedness Confusion or difficulty concentrating Dry skin Headaches Ref: Also, not listed there, as a woman, I can tell a difference with my menstrual cycle too. Your post helped me reflect on my prescribed Adderral history. Thank you. Not sure if this story helps or not, but I am wondering what the reaction is in our bodies after decades of Adderral usage? My dad popped Aleves back in the day like candy because it was supposedly safe. Now he's 83 and his gut lining is trashed from years of innocent NSAID OTC use. After my divorce 11 years ago, I was overprescribed in dosage wrt adderral and also got scripts to cope with other mental issues: depression, anxiety, insomnia - my doctor was a general practitioner in South Dakota, not strict rules back then, and I just didn't know what I didn't know. I got up to 45 XR a day on Adderral in about three months along with Xanax, Klonapin, Valium and moreвЂ¦ The combination of the meds and not taking good care of myself spun out of control b/c I was also drinking lots of caffeine and alcohol everyday too also Adderral no nos (coping with divorce was hard mentally and physically), so my kidneys and my liver were stressed out as much as my head was too. On top of frequent UTIs the side effects just kept ramping up. After about 6 months I quit all Rx meds for depression, insomnia, anxiety - as well as the Adderall. I detoxed on my own: ROUGH. But I got through it. Four years ago, perimenopause made my ADHD so much worse. I decided to go back on Adderall and my new psych doctor prescribed 10mg instant release - base dose - and that was too much for me: I had a headache with severe anger mood swings. My doc dropped the dose to 5 mg, yet that was not enough. We discovered 7.5 mg is my sweet spot. I haven't had to increase the dosage in 3 years and am allowed to take 3.25 mg - 7.5 mg in the afternoon as needed to manage ADHD. I don't take off days unless I forget. So my long winded point: it's up to us to know our bodies, check our dose, and consider the impacts of long term usage. Keep on keeping on! ?????United! Now where'd I leave my damned water bottle? ??

text: I'm probably going to make a post about this in the thread if I'm allowed but if you can afford it, I would suggest: 1: Check your labels! Generic brand companies are only required to have x amount of the main active ingredient in their formulas and any/all inactive ingredients can affect how the medicine dispenses in your body. How do I know this? I took an intro to pharmacology and it explicitly said this in the first couple chapters. If you have a generic from Amneal: in my experience it's complete crap and does not work. I personally had every side effect known to man with it and this is not the only medicine I've had that issue with from that labeler/manufacturer but once I switched to a different one, it worked so much better. 2. If switching to a different manufacturer of the generic doesn't work, i would probably ask if you can try the brand only of the medicine (if you can afford it). Generics aren't allowed to make exactly as the brand does which is why all the inactive ingredients often are different and again, they change how the medicine works. Talk to your doctor. 3. I would say if none of the above worked, then try either upping or lowering the dose at the discretion of your doctor. 4. If none of the above works, only then I would say to try something else and then follow the list again to ensure if you're getting what you need from your medicine and tell your doctor of any changes. 5. Finally, if all else fails and you still don't feel like it's helping, consider that there might be something more going on. ADHD is an issue that causes so many other symptoms in everyone that sometimes the issue you're experiencing might actually be the issue. Example: feeling depressed all the time vs adhd depression. Again, talk to your doctor So much of this is self reflection and being able to identify what is going on within ourselves. For me, writing down or talking notes when I remembered helped to identify my good days from my bad days and I could bring that to my therapist and take what we talked about to my psych to explore different things it could be. Remember every person is different and we all have different experiences that affect us just as differently but we are not alone. Please note: I am not a medical professional nor do I claim to be one, but these are things I've learned over the years of being medicated for my adhd, working with both my therapist and psych and the things I've also read and studied in various college medical courses, so always always talk to your doctor about what is (potentially) right for you. Thanks for coming to my Ted talk. Good luck!
predicted_span: I'm probably going to make a post about this in the thread if I'm allowed but if you can afford it, I would suggest: 1: Check your labels!

text: No, you don't stop taking either. "Running on anxiety" works for a while, until you burn out. It was destined to stop working at some point. Now, you start using an agenda and a small notebook as your "second brain" and you try to find tricks to make you do the stuff you need to do. Such as "counting to three" and then getting up, listening to music every waking hour, making "going to sleep sufficiently early in order to sleep a minimum of 8 hours" the highest priority task, exercising everytime you remember to do it (everytime it crosses your mind, you do 5 proper squats or wall push-ups), taking over the counter basic multivitamin-minerals pills (12 vitamins and 8 minerals should be good) or (even better) getting a general blood test, etc.

text: I only worked one job, but I had to quit due to the pressures it was putting on me. Long story short, the stress got so extreme that I was hospitalized for observation. This was in 2002. I haven't worked since then. As such, I also have no real money. I sort of have a mild drug addiction. I take prescription drugs for various health problems along with some over-the-counter drugs for other issues. I try not to overdose on them as best as I can. I don't have any gambling addiction that I'm aware of. But, my porn addiction is **EXTREME!** What's worse is that depression, anxiety, insomnia, and health problems often (though, not always) trigger me to engage in this addiction. The last time I was in love with someone was also back in 2002 with my last girlfriend. But, things didn't work out for us, and we ended our relationship. I haven't found anyone else since then. The only friends I have are online and live too far away. I don't have any real friends that I could hang out and socialize with...not that I could in the miserable condition I'm in today. I've also failed at connecting with people, except with my dad (whom I currently live with). He and my mom separated when I was just a baby. I don't actually remember this. That's just what I was told by my dad and mom. I have no talent and, because of my miserable existence, it's impossible for me to be social. text: I'm so sorry for the response you've been getting from doctors. It sounds to me like you might have GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease). For various reasons, stomach acid and sometimes food and drink from the stomach can escape up into the esophagus. It can be painful, burning, and can come with a lot of other symptoms. The egg white clear stuff is most likely mucous that your body is making as a means of protecting your esophagus from the stomach acid. Some things you can do to get some relief, avoid eating and drinking a lot of fluid within a couple hours of bedtime. Avoid spicy foods and heavy, greasy meals (especially later in the evening). Avoid overeating, but especially in the evening. If your symptoms most bother you at night or in the early morning, try sleeping on an incline, either by raising the head of your bed or by using pillows to prop yourself up. If you're in the US, you can also buy medicine over the counter, maybe try Pepcid AC (Famotidine) and take that a few hours before bed for a couple weeks and see if that helps with your symptoms.
predicted_span: I'm so sorry for the response you've been getting from doctors.

It was really scary and I was in so much pain. I had written notes on my phone because I have medical trauma, PTSD and autism and was worried about a verbal shutdown which often happens. I kept trying to get people to read it and they would just hand it back and say that I should tell them in my own words right then the one single thing I was there for today. The nurse who was there when the paramedics did the hand-off was the one that I told part of my story to while she tried to start an IV. She was super sweet and reassured me that the help I had been waiting so many years for was finally here. I've been on a journey of remembering my own life. I've always had significant amnesia. Now I know why. It was so so terrible. I was in captivity for years by a man I thought I loved... because I couldn't remember the terrifying night he date r@ped me or when he moved in without my consent and then used drugs to convince me I was crazy and he was my loving partner who was caring for me. I was super super sick at this time as well and had zero support outside of him. Today my body FELT and remembered the dozens and dozens of times he r@ped me while I laid there sobbing. I felt shock and horror. Some parts of me knew that my daughters father was my r@pist but another part found out today and they are not taking it well. The emotional flashback was triggered by my fear of something happening to me and my r@pist getting sole custody of my daughter. I have no idea what time is. I don't experience it's passage anymore. I'm just in pain every moment and my brain offers up the idea to just give up and die several times a day at least. I have severe amnesia right now so I've been writing things down and photographing them. Only by checking my photos did I find out that I'd been vomiting blood recently. I often don't remember anything I've done in the past 8 months or sometimes ever, but other moments I remember everything and then I usually find myself screaming uncontrollably. So back to today... the nurse who I told my story to disappeared and I never saw her again. Apparently she wasn't my nurse. My nurse didn't talk to me besides just telling me what she was doing. I was crying a lot of the time, shivering, cold, and in 10/10 pain. I laid there thinking over and over please God let this be it. I'm ready for it to be over. I just want to go. Or at least have a few hours of relief. The doctor comes in while I'm in a spasm. I can't speak hardly as it's hard to control my breathing. I'm too tired to breathe and want to stop. The doc gruffly asked why I was there. I tried to start to tell him and he cut me off. He said no no, what is the ONE reason you are here. I manage to reply pain. He says where. I say everywhere and start to elaborate to list it out and explain it all. He cuts me off and asks if my belly hurts. I say yes. He pushes everywhere and asks me to find the one spot that hurts the most out of the painful spots. I'm confused because we are completely off track and it's like he's blind and hasn't spoken to a single other person who had assessed me. Also I'm confused because pain and again having a super hard time holding it together and not full blown hyperventilating because it's so so bad. He orders an abdominal CT and a few labs. I get Zofran and Toradol which does absolutely nothing for my pain levels. At this point I've been crying for hours. For a while I even found myself on the floor of the bathroom with the light off crying my heart out very loudly. When someone found me they just said get up and get back in bed and they left. My crying was so intense it set off a series of crushing chest pains. So I try to stop crying. It's more of a whimper and quiet voice yell as I draw out each exhale and focus on my breath. This intensity of pain is like laboring was. The doctor comes in and says good news! All your results look great and you're being discharged...Follow up with this gastroenterologist for your GI problems. Completely ignoring the severe lethargy, extreme difficulty ambulating, kidney pain, altered mental status, chest pain, asthma attacks worsening and current meds not helping, inability to care for self, pain that makes my brain wish I was dead. Yay! Great news. Labs are good. I don't have a pcp or care team at all. I've been in survival mode every single day since maybe forever. I've literally begged for an admission to the hospital and I've been gaslit and ignored. When I dragged myself out of the hospital in my socks in the rain I wanted to just go lay down in the road. I wish I would have un@lived myself before I became a mother. No way in hell I'm leaving her without a fight, but goddammit why won't anyone help me? I don't need another phone number for a doctor with a 3 month wait list, or to be told to go to the DV agency when the local one didn't help me already. I need a safe bed and some actual sleep and actual quality medical care. I'm going to have a heart attack if I can't lower my pain to a level of stress I can tolerate or drink myself to death. Idk. I've been to dozens of doctors, lived in hospitals for probably 6 months if you add it up, trialed over 100 drugs, and costs well over 400k at this point. It's hard to feel like there is any hope left. I guess I'm posting this into the void so if I succumb someone can piece it together if anyone cares after I'm gone.

text: I do some things to help with my ADHD (I'm forgetful, can't remember if I've taken meds, what I'm supposed to do for the dayвЂ¦). I got myself a weekly pill organizer. Mine has morning, afternoon, and nighttime pill slots. Each day is removable so I put the day on top of the pill case so I can quickly see it and know if I have taken my meds or not for that day. And the case zips up so I can just zip it up and take it with me when I travel. Life changing. I also use the hell out of some calendars. I put everything in my phone calendar. I also have a white board monthly calendar on my fridge where I transfer everything from my phone to so I can see it throughout the day. Everything is color coordinated and the colors on the phone match the fridge. Something that may help you is when you schedule out your day, schedule out at least one event per day that works on your self care. Maybe you do one of those foot peel masks, or a mask for your face, or do a hair mask after your shower? Bubble bath? Trim/paint your nails? Little things that can make you feel good and are part of caring for yourself. You deserve to treat yourself well and it may help keep you from getting too low. I don't know what type of support system you have, but it's time to lean on them. They can't help you if they don't know what is going on. Also, if they care about you, they likely have been waiting for you to come to them for help. This will not be a surprise to the people who love you. Everyone needs help sometimes. Wishing you the best, OP.

text: If it's concerning, definitely see a doctor. But know that headaches have many causes, and so many of them are trivial. The temple region especially, I'm not a doctor to be clear but its a common sight for aches and pains. The jaw joint is there as well as a plethora of muscles and stuff. Ear injuries/infections/pain can even be felt in the temples. Referred pain is also a thing, it's actually normal to have wisdom teeth pain and for that pain to be felt elsewhere in the head, like the temples. (Hence the term *referred* pain) all this to say, there's a million reasons from eye strain, teeth clenching, dehydration, migraines and even anxiety being a dicc. Tension headaches can be quite painful too, don't underestimate them. Best advice is to rest in a dark, quiet room with some ice or a heat pack and over the counter pain meds and cold water. Try to get some sleep too, it really helps with headaches and especially migraines. I definitely recommend seeing a doctor especially if the pain is new.
predicted_span: If it's concerning, definitely see a doctor. But know that headaches have many causes, and so many of them are trivial.

text: yeah that's what makes meds so tricky, it's a different experience for everyone based on body chemistry. Buspar made me feel the same way so my PCP cut my morning dose in half which was just enough to calm me down without the brain fog/fatigue. My PM dose is full though and makes me SLUMPED instead of having spiraling thoughts and insomnia which is why I like it. There's a test that can be done through GeneSight, it's a saliva test, and it tells you what meds work best for your body chemistry, which are O.K. and may need some adjusting, and which are not a good fit at all- and gives reasons why/identification of your genes and any mutations. It's how I found out I'm homozygous for MTHFR gene mutation which causes all sorts of reactions to a bunch of medications. It identifies for all drug classifications including OTC meds. It definitely shortens the lengthy annoying experimentation phase of finding meds/doses. It's like $330 but less if you have insurance that'll cover some.

text: Over here the collection area tends to be right Infront of the dispensary area, so the main work area/bench/computers are overlooking out to the area where patients collect their prescriptions or ask for advice and buy otc medicines (prescriptions are free here but lots of people want to talk to the pharmacist for advice and recommendations) The dispensary assistants and counter staff usually take the patients name when they ask for their script, go get it from where it's bagged on the shelf waiting to be collected, bring it to the pharmacist to ok that it's the right one and good to go out before handing it out to the patient, or letting the pharmacist hand it out if there is any counselling needed such as this is a new item, these are the specific directions, your dose has changed etc. Our CDs need to stay in the safe at all times until the patient is collecting them, most places I've worked in the will label the item but keep the labels in a separate pile to the side, then prepare it when the patient comes in to collect it, then enter it out of the CD register at the time of collection, that way it's easier to keep a track of the running balance and also once it's been entered out as given to a patient and then if they don't collect it can't be entered back in again, even if they didn't collect - so it's wasted. We do have patient packs (little box with strips of tablets - usually 28/56 depending on the dose - so each box/pack is usually a month's supply) for everything really and it's very rare to use stock bottles and need to count stuff. Interesting how places do things so differently.

text: Well, to start, there are lots of different medications for almost all conditions. There are a lot of different blood pressure meds, not just one kind. Googling comes up with 8 different categories of blood pressure medications, with multiple different drugs in each different category. Heck, even when you're talking about pain relievers, over the counter you can get aspirin, acetaminophen, naproxen, or ibuprofen, and that's completely separate from prescription pain killers. In any case, ADHD meds work on your brain and its neurotransmitters, and brain chemistry is especially complicated and difficult to pin down. FWIW, there are genetic markers for how well you process different kinds of drugs. (I did gene testing that shows that methylphenidate is less likely to be effective, which was true - I didn't get any effect from methylphenidate. Adderall and Vyvanse both work, though.) Everyone's genetic material is different so there are a lot of different ways that different people will process medications.
predicted_span: Well, to start, there are lots of different medications for almost all conditions.

text: Working in a country with universal healthcare (as an RN), I truly could not fathom for our patients, having the extra stressors of the financial burdens of healthcare. I recognize its exorbitant costs, and the time/effort of physicians and other specialists that comprise the system to treat, manage, and "deal" with difficult and frequently recurring patients. I absolutely would agree with others' statements that this "right" to healthcare could be bolstered by much stronger regulations in the public health sector in terms of creating more accessible healthy food options, stronger education about health maintenance and prevention, as well as, robust government intervention in medication costs In terms of compensation, ethics, and principles. Absolutely people should be compensated for their education, and we should have programming in place to aid in subsidizing these costs, as it is not only financial, but time. Losing out on 12 years of life to provide care for others deserves that appreciation and respect. As with other things? It has not been uncommon for me to witness "hey doc, I have had this patient before, I truly do not believe I would be providing the best level of care I could given circumstances x,y,z. I would be more than happy to help you with a case or transfer a patient to me if that would be more agreeable with you." Otherwise? Yes. Human beings were never 'asked' to be here, we simply just came to be, by the actions of others. Do I believe that people have to be accountable and responsible for their health? Absolutely. Returning back to the robust preventative care though, it is important, I believe, to recognize the role that our societies, our cultures, our values, and the ever shifting landscape of our day-to-day lives as an important aspect in our view of health. Why should my regular mid-life patient with EtOH abuse be turned away, when looking at the generational trauma, lack of community, familial dysfunction, and lack of supports that we can provide in our communities? Why should we, as human beings, look at a poor migrant family with poor health literacy, who did not understand how we could use OTC medications to manage a fever, does not understand how to access and utilize resources in a new society, and are fearful for their febrile, but otherwise, healthy child? Looking at healthcare through the lens of what it represents to me, is looking not at the "costs of doing business," but at what "costs" we are able to protect by aiding people in returning to work, being able to assess and protect finite resources through honest discussions around health goals, and levels of care, and through prevention that we can produce with education. Is universal healthcare at times, sticky? Of course. Are there people who abuse the system? Yes. Is there at time, a lack of health literacy, or strong fears of mortality that cause frequent fliers to return requiring the same teaching and guidance many times? Yes. But, by-in-large? My experience working on Med-Surg, mental health, corrections and ED have shown me that most times? These are simply people who, are someone's loved one. A son, a daughter, father, mother, aunt, their lifeline, their reason to live, their caregivers, their best friend. And if I was in their shoes? Even if things are not always perfect. I would hope that the people who are taking care of me, recognize the value of my life, that I deserve to be there, and that, I too, just want to live and be able to return to a 'normal' life after being ill. Sorry about the tangent, and if it went a little rollercoaster style, I was battling with a puppy
predicted_span: Working in a country with universal healthcare (as an RN), I truly could not fathom for our patients, having the extra stressors of the financial burdens of healthcare.

text: Start with minimum medical dosage - I'd consult my doc about this, and increase after a minimum of a week if there are no nasty side effects. So many other riskier medications. I switched from sertraline (SSRI) to venlafaxine (SNRI) once the former lost its' effectiveness. While the SNRI was a right choice to switch to and helped greatly over time, I did take Xanax over a week or two, due to nervousness as a side effect (the N standing for noradrenaline in SNRI). Never needed with the sertraline SSRI. So while SNRI helped me more it also initially gave more side effects. And most antihistamines - either first gen like Atarax (allergies and sedative effect potentially used for anxiety) or second gen, nonsedating like Allegra. Fenoxyfenadine was it? This one OTC. Actually dangerous if used wrongly? Benzos. Barbiturates were even worse btw. ADHD stimulants- Ritalin. Adderall. If you choose to abuse those. Antidepressants that CAN be dangerous? Bupropion (due to lowering seizure treshold mostly). MAOIs (interactions with other meds and even with some foods). Stuff one can indeed turn down SSRIs for? For me, emotional blunting and libido loss/sexual problems. No crazy stuff like addiction overdoses heart problems liver damage...name it. Best of luck! Doc is there to reassure you remember. text: Now tell me, how in the fuck do you do 800 a day? By sacrificing patient care in order to meet the corporate-mandated metrics, like the OP prescriber detailed. The staffing pharmacist and one (or, if we were lucky, two) technician would come in half an hour before the pharmacy opened in order to fill the queue of refill requests that piled up overnight. As a technician, I had a handheld scanner that I would use to scan the script then the drug and I'd count out the appropriate amount, then bottle and label. Everything goes into a basket and set on the counter for the pharmacist to check. Uninterrupted like this, we could get through maybe 60-100 scripts, depending on the pharmacist and the technician (not to gloat, but I was the fastest and most accurate technician in our store's district, according to the metrics I still loathe). Then we'd open properly and the other technician and the cashier would come in. The cashier strictly pulled the finished dispenses and rang out patients. The other technician worked the dropoff window where patients would bring hard copy scripts and where we resolved any insurance discrepancies/denials, triaged phone calls, etc. This would continue for a few hours (without breaks) until the second pharmacist came in. The first pharmacist would then go to lunch while the second took over checking. There would be a brief period, usually an hour or so, where we had two pharmacists checking, which would (hopefully) get us caught up. Then the second pharmacist would take their lunch and when they came back, it was time for the first pharmacist to leave. By this point we'd usually done something like two-thirds of our fills for the day. Because we were doing so many scripts in such a short amount of time, that leaves literally no room for the pharmacist to do such things as verify controlled substances, counsel patients (most refused it anyway, but we were required to offer it), or advise on OTC products. All the pharmacists were able to do was check fills the technicians queued up for them. As a tech, I handled all the insurance rejections, copay questions, phone calls for refills that could have been put on through the automated system, patients coming up the window requesting a refill on a medication they don't know the name of, don't know what they're taking it for, and only vaguely know what it looks like, etc. All while dodging insults and threats (and more often than you might think, objects) hurled at me for a patient's medication not being ready when they wanted it to be. All for $2 USD over minimum wage. Usually after 17:00 things would slow down, so we could (again) catch up on the backlogged queue of new/refill scripts and start on EOD procedures like filing hard copy scripts and placing the order. Staff would stay for half an hour or so after closing to finish out whatever we could, knowing it would never be enough and we're still going to get bitched at by corporate for not pushing immunizations hard enough or some shit. Yeah, I don't miss retail.

text: Even in U.S where you need a script even for... Hydroxyzine ? Where I live (in EU), in my country all meds (except narcotics and antibiotics) are OTC. If we want to get a med cheap AF, we go to a public doctor (public servant) you pay nothing for the visit, they e-prescribe and later at the pharmacy, you give them the printed prescription and pay only 25% if insured, and completely nothing if uninsured. Otherwise, we go to a pharmacy, ask for the med and mgs we want, pay it 100% its price and voila, mission complete. Most citizens here going in the prescription route, are usually the elderly or when it's a prescription for benzos, opis or stims (or antibiotics) or when we want very expensive meds. The state pays them for us. Including the Bupe substitution clinic. The clinic is free as well (and state-operated) and unlimited subs supply, free for life. Methadone is banned though for some years now, but slowly in some clinics they are starting again to use it.

text: In the U.K. we limit the amount of pain relief that can be bought from a shop, such as a convenience store or grocery store, this applies to Paracetamol, Ibuprofen and Aspirin. You can buy in any shop, in a single transaction - this is what we call the General Sales List (GSL) 2 boxes of 16 Paracetamol 500mg Or 2 boxes of 16 ibuprofen 200mg Or 2 Boxes of 16 Aspirin 75mg/150mg/300mg In a pharmacy you can buy - 2 boxes of 32 Paracetamol 500mg but at the pharmacist discretion for people with chronic conditions they can sell upto 100 500mg tablets. More than 100 tablets requires a prescription. You can buy ibuprofen in 200mg or 400mg strength largest pack size being 96. There isn't a limit but again it's at the pharmacist discretion. Most won't supply more than 100 without a prescription. Also with a prescription you can get it in 200mg/400mg/600mg/800mg. You can buy Aspirin in 75mg 28 or 100 tablets. 150mg 28 or 32. 300mg 28 or 32. Again there isn't a limit but it is at the pharmacist discretion. You can get higher strengths but you need a prescription for that.

text: Your doctor is a liar. Okay, I never share my age, but I got diagnosed last year when I was 49 YO. I didn't go into the evaluation with a intended solution like I do with my medical doctor, I went to find out if my suspicions were correct and if the behaviors I had always done to mask it were legit. The reason for the evaluation was that I had gotten COVID and it messed up my thinking and my physical "stats" so to speak( in game terms, like perception, endurance, focus) and literally before Covid, I suspected but I was doin all these tasks and systems to be a succesful adult and so I didnt NEED anything else, but all that came crashing down after Covid exposed me. I went into the evaluation honestly, just to find out. The results were sent to my PCP and the Psych doctors recommendation, despite all I told them about how I usually cope, was to start me on stimulant medication. I didn't ASK for it. I didnt even Mention it. It was their recommendation after evaluation. So, "They" DO prescribe medication for adult ADHD. Their justification was that whatever I had been doing to cope wasn't working anymore and my lifestyle , job and health were at risk if I were unable to regain the minimum amount of function I had previously. Don't feel bad to express how much you are suffering. It is embarrasing, you will want to play it down to not look "pathetic" but the lowest of your lows is what they need to KNOW about you in order to gauge what might be the problem, what might help and what won't. If you had a burn out, Tell them everything you do to try to seem "normal". What systems do you use? Do you use multiple alarms, Do you have a timer in every room of your house? Do you write notes to your self and stick them all over the house? Must you label a drawer on the outside to remember what is inside? Do you have a mental tantrum if something you usually use in one place is moved to another? Can you start a task and get distracted to a totally different task that has a commonprecursor to the first ? (e.g. I need to clear the counter, so I move dishes to the sink and start water, but then instead of going back to the counter to move more things, I start washing dishes instead. The counter is still cluttered and I cannot use it for the project I intended to do) or ( e.g. My boss asks me to review the results of the tests for 400 participants, and as I look I realize a pattern, so I spend the rest of the day writing a formula to highlight that pattern and show the result which , btw, accomplishes the same outcome, but was not what I was tasked to do) Do you have trouble with the transition required to take a shower/bath? Do you stay up all night because something caught your attention? Do you struggle with yourself to not act impulsively? Are you a unredeemable people pleaser? Do you struggle to read large blocks of texts but can listen to audio books? or do voices sound like noise but you can read forever? everyone is different, but that doesn't change how difficult is is to process what is difficult for YOU. These kind of things reck havoc on your self care, your mental focus, and your ability to follow through on tasks. The Psych doc needs to know this stuff. Sorry I blabbered. I just remembered how exposed I felt with these strangers who told me to just relax and be myself and breaking through my boundaries of comfort, I did and I was so embarrassed but they saw ME. You need to find a doctor who is willing to SEE You.
predicted_span: Your doctor is a liar. Okay, I never share my age, but I got diagnosed last year when I was 49 YO.

"The Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL) refers to the highest level of daily nutrient intake that is likely to pose no risk of adverse health effects to almost all individuals in the general population. As intake increases above the UL, the risk of adverse effects increases. The term tolerable is chosen because it connotes a level of intake that can, with high probability, be tolerated biologically by individuals; it does not imply acceptability of that level in any other sense. The setting of a UL does not indicate that nutrient intakes greater than the Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) or Adequate Intake (AI) are recommended as being beneficial to an individual. Many individuals are self-medicating with nutrients for curative or treatment purposes. It is beyond the scope of this report to address the possible therapeutic benefits of higher nutrient intakes that may offset the risk of adverse effects. The UL is not meant to apply to individuals who are treated with the nutrient under medical supervision. It is designed to be applied to almost all individuals in the general healthy population." National Library of Medicine
